title: How Quantum Computing Is Breaking Modern Encryption

slides.heading: Quantum Computing vs. Encryption
slides.body: How the next computing revolution threatens the security of everything online

slides.heading: Encryption Keeps the Internet Safe
slides.body: Most online security relies on RSA and ECC encryption. These work because factoring huge numbers or solving elliptic curve problems takes classical computers thousands of years — making brute-force attacks practically impossible.

slides.heading: What Makes Quantum Computers Different
slides.body: Unlike classical bits (0 or 1), quantum bits (qubits) exist in superposition — simultaneously 0 and 1. This lets quantum computers explore many solutions at once, giving them exponential speedups on specific problems.

slides.heading: Shor's Algorithm: The Encryption Killer
slides.body: In 1994, mathematician Peter Shor devised an algorithm that lets a quantum computer factor large numbers exponentially faster than any classical method. A sufficiently large quantum computer running Shor's algorithm could crack RSA encryption in hours.

slides.heading: How Far Away Is the Threat?
slides.body: Today's quantum computers are too small and error-prone to break real encryption. Experts estimate a cryptographically relevant quantum computer — needing millions of stable qubits — is roughly 10–15 years away, though timelines are uncertain.

slides.heading: 'Harvest Now, Decrypt Later' Attacks
slides.body: Nation-states are already collecting encrypted data today with plans to decrypt it once quantum computers mature. Sensitive government, financial, and medical records stolen now could be exposed in the future — making the threat urgent even before quantum computers arrive.

slides.heading: Post-Quantum Cryptography to the Rescue
slides.body: NIST finalized its first post-quantum cryptography standards in 2024, including CRYSTALS-Kyber and CRYSTALS-Dilithium. These algorithms are designed to resist both classical and quantum attacks, based on hard lattice math problems.

slides.heading: Big Tech Is Already Migrating
slides.body: Google, Apple, and Signal have begun integrating post-quantum algorithms into their products. Google Chrome now uses a hybrid classical/post-quantum key exchange. The migration of global internet infrastructure will take years but is already underway.

slides.heading: Quantum Key Distribution: Unbreakable by Physics
slides.body: QKD uses quantum mechanics itself to share encryption keys — any eavesdropper disturbs the quantum state and is immediately detected. China's Micius satellite has demonstrated QKD over 1,200 km, pointing toward a quantum-secure internet.

slides.heading: The Clock Is Ticking
slides.body: Quantum computing promises to upend encryption as we know it. The good news: the cryptographic community is ready. Transitioning to post-quantum standards now — before quantum computers arrive — is the key to staying secure in the quantum era.
